text_snippet: "Our public services must be funded through a taxation system that is fair, sustainable and just. Sinn Féin would also ensure that our tax base is sustainable, improves our public services, delivers for workers and families, and protects the public finances."

source_type: Older national or European election manifestos
text_snippet: "Sinn Féin priorities: » Funding and implementing the National Maternity Strategy with an additional €75 million » A greater focus on care and diagnosis for cardiovascular disease in women » Education and awareness programmes for breast and cervical cancer and the repatriation of screening services » Free contraceptives, in all forms, and emergency contraception » Enacting safe access zones"

source_type: Older national or European election manifestos
text_snippet: "Sinn Féin is determined to avoid the failed policies that have fostered resentments and tensions in other countries. Successive Governments have failed to deliver what is needed in terms of housing and health delivery. They and they alone are responsible for their failures. Sinn Féin does not want open borders. We believe that all states must manage migration. Every state has to have an immigration system with well-functioning rules and regulation that everyone understands and that serves the interest of the people of the country. This system must have regard to how many people are needed to meet shortfalls in the labour market and how many people can be integrated effectively with adequate support and resourcing. Where we do need migrants, such as to fill vacancies in our health system, our migration system should facilitate this. The system must also protect people fleeing persecution and war, our international obligations must be fulfilled. We should end the Direct Provision system, treat people with dignity, process applications for asylum in a timely manner and implement the recommendations of the All-Party Oireachtas Committee Report."

source_type: Older national or European election manifestos

text_snippet: "We will oppose the further militarisation of the EU and attempts to create a standing EU army. We would terminate Ireland’s involvement in EU Battle Groups and the use of Irish airports by foreign armies engaged in war. This Fine Gael Government, with Fianna Fáil support, has approved Ireland’s involvement in the European Union’s Permanent Structured Cooperation (PESCO), which is the beginning of an EU Defence Union and the creation of an EU Army. Sinn Féin opposed the creation of PESCO and, in Government, we will ensure Ireland plays absolutely no part in PESCO. We propose to hold a referendum on enshrining neutrality into Bunreacht na hÉireann. The referendum would decide whether to amend the constitution to ensure Ireland will not and cannot aid foreign powers in any way in preparation for a war. The proposed amendment would also affirm that Ireland is a neutral state and that the State would have a policy of non-membership of military alliances."

text_snippet: "Sinn Féin believes that the transition to ecological sustainability must be framed by principles of social justice and equality. We need a just transition to a carbon-free future. If the process is not fair, neither will the outcome, that is for certain. Fine Gael and Fianna Fail want to leave it to market forces. They want us to believe that structural change can be achieved through leaflets and ad campaigns. source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: "Below we summarise the main measures we would take to even out the resources created in Ireland. - Close corporation tax loopholes and double the tax take. Ireland’s official rate of 12.5% is too low – but corporations do not even pay this. Because of a host of loopholes, the effective rate is between 4-5%. This is far lower than an average worker would pay. Corporations should pay a minimum rate of 20% on their profits – which is what low paid workers pay on their wages. But it should also be pushed up higher. - Make Apple pay their back taxes. In 2016 the EU Commission exposed Ireland as a tax haven. Deals made by the Irish Revenue Commissioners in 1991 and 2007 allowed Apple to get away with a tax rate of less than 2%. They now owe the Irish people €13 billion, plus interest – a total of €18-19 billion. This would go a long way to developing an indigenous development strategy but instead, the Irish government is spending millions in order to refuse the money. People Before Profit would drop all legal cases and take the money immediately. - A Tax on Millionaires. People Before Profit would levy a tax of 2% on the top 5% of wealth holders. Before this would kick in, however, we would allow one million euros to be set aside for their family home. This would affect the richest 18,000 households in the state, each of whom has roughly €3 million in accumulated wealth. The tax would bring in roughly €3.25 billion annually. - New Taxes on High Earners. People Before Profit would introduce new tax bands on those lucky enough to earn more than €100,000 per annum. The table below shows the initiative."

Given that refugees are people who have had to flee their own countries, to arrive here and be left in camps for years instead of their cases being dealt with efficiently is abhorrent, and utterly disregards their humanity and circumstances. Often these are families who cannot live a normal family life while held in a Direct Provision centre. The Irish establishment is great at looking to the past to find sources of shame, however our generation has many sources of shame now that are simply ignored. Processing time for refugees needs to be reduced significantly in order to allow people to live in dignity while they await decisions. Mismanaged policy in this area can needlessly develop opposition, especially given that the government is both failing to deliver on its immigration objectives and is also failing to work in partnership with local communities. source_type: Recent interviews, press releases and social media communication (from party, party leader and/or leading candidates)
text_snippet: Labour supports Ireland remaining a militarily neutral country, but supports ongoing aid to Ukraine. See Party Leader statement from 2023 first anniversary of the war: Deputy Bacik said: “The Labour Party stands in full solidarity with the people of Ukraine following the brutal and unprovoked Russian invasion. One year on, we continue to receive horrific, unthinkable news of the acts of aggression and brutal acts of warfare committed by Putin’s regime. Russia has displaced entire communities, destroyed their homes, and continues to put lives at risk. It is completely unacceptable. “We have to pay tribute to the immense courage and resilience of the Ukrainian people. Today, we remember the lives lost, their family, friends and communities. We stand with the people of Ukraine and we are reiterating our calls to the Irish Government to continue to condemn this horrific war, and double down on our efforts to support those fleeing Putin here in Ireland. “While we in the Labour Party have welcomed the rounds of EU sanctions and welcomed the expulsion by the Government last year of four diplomatic staff from the Russian embassy, we believe stronger measures need to be taken. “Indeed, President Zelenskyy in his address to the Oireachtas last year called on us to do that and told us our leadership can make a difference. I am renewing my call today for the expulsion of the Russian ambassador from Ireland as he continues to mount brazen denials of the truth of what is happening in Ukraine and what his government is doing to the people of Ukraine. “Putin has shown that he wants to wipe Ukraine off the map. He wants to abolish Ukraine’s culture and its history. He must fail and he will fail. We are a militarily neutral country, and we are very proud in the Labour Party of defending our military neutrality, but we must not and cannot be politically neutral in the face of this brutal aggression from the Russian leadership. “Ireland should be proud at the immense solidarity and generosity communities have shown in welcoming the 77,000 Ukrainian refugees who have come to our shores. The Government needs to show a more co-ordinated approach in meeting the needs of refugees in the communities that are welcoming them. We need a strong public information campaign. “We stand with Ukraine. We stand with Ukraine in Ireland. Slava Ukraini.”

source_type: 2024 EP election manifesto of the party
text_snippet: Right to Health: Everyone residing in the EU deserves access to quality healthcare. This includes: - Drug Users’ Rights: Recognize the right to health for people who use drugs, including those with addiction and those exploring consciousness through non-impulsive rational agency. - Peer-to-Peer Clinics: License peer-to-peer clinics to provide safe access to plant-based substances for communities.

I propose a universal payment accessible to everyone that is means tested based on contributed taxes and historical salary. There is a need to maintain an incentive to work, particularly for our youth, fostering a healthy work ethic.

Government spending ought to prioritise the most important areas such as public health. Tax reforms, comprehensive audits of publicly funded bodies, and reduced government expenses are essential to ensure efficient use of taxpayer money.

Maintain the current tax rate for high-income earners. Instead of burdening individuals who contribute to the economy in various ways, the focus should be on taxing large corporations more effectively striking a fair balance and promote economic growth in more ways than one.

While acknowledging the need to review the pension system, I firmly oppose any increase in the retirement age. Although people are living longer, this longevity should not translate into more work years. Quality of life matters, and people should not feel compelled to work beyond their capacity. Those who opt to continue working beyond retirement age should receive benefits, and be valued for their continued contributions to society. There should also be protections for self-employed people who often can't avail of retirement due to financial pressure

Policy should aim to protect essential commodities from price increases through a reduction in sales tax on basic necessities such as fuel for residential heating and essential nutritional foods like eggs, milk, and grains. Ensuring affordability, prioritising well-being and alleviating financial burdens on citizens is a net benfit.
Marriage should be open to consenting adults, irrespective of sexual orientation, and the privileges associated with marriage should be equally accessible without discrimination.

Naturalization into society should not be compulsory. The majority of adults born in Ireland do not speak the national language. However, maintaining language requirements for young people applying to university and encouraging more spoken Irish in society is important. There are other ways to allow the adoption of cultural values.

Ensuring access to abortion should extend beyond the sole responsibility of General Practitioners. Adequate funding for specialized clinics, along with peer support networks run by women, is crucial to effectively address this need. It is essential that women do not have to emigrate to access healthcare due to policies influenced by morality.

Striking a balance between open borders and restrictive measures is important. Efforts should focus on mitigating the root cause of mass migration; war and conflict. Additionally, welcoming skilled labor into the country can significantly contribute to the economy.

A job position should be filled by the most qualified candidate, irrespective of gender. However, research shows that greater diversity contributes to well-rounded decision-making. To promote better decision-making there should be an equal quota for male and female in executive positions within publicly funded bodies, including representation in Dáil Éireann and the EU Parliament.

Taxes should be directed toward improving public infrastructure including railroads, bus services, and cycle lanes. However, the shift toward greener alternatives cannot rely solely on taxation. It is imperative to provide viable alternatives for people than driving cars.

text_snippet: o the President, the people of Ireland won the right to proper, safe, and free abortions in the year 2018 (two thousand and eighteen). This happened after years of fighting against constitutional prohibition, and a situation where women had to travel abroad to get abortions, but not at home. People were tired of this hypocritical pretense, pretending that the Magdalene Laundries and the Mother and Baby Homes never existed. The referendum victory showed that people rightly understood that society must trust women and support them in whatever choice they make, facing crisis without planning. Abortion should be part of the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union.

source_type: Recent interviews, press releases and social media communication (from party, party leader and/or leading candidates)
text_snippet: "Mr President, for months now in Ireland, the media and the politicians have been obsessed with talk of immigration: we have to have an ‘honest’ conversation about migration, they tell us we have no choice in the matter at all. Funny enough now though, they’re very good at ignoring things when they want to. For example, if you’re demanding a right to housing or healthcare or disability rights, well then they don’t want to know. But if you’re talking about immigration, then you’re not talking about those things. You’re not talking about how your state is serving transnational capital rather than the citizens. In reality, it is the great deceit. In Britain, for example, they’ve been consumed by a conversation about migration for more than a decade now. Savage austerity? Well, it got right off the hook. So, while they were being ‘honest’ about migration, child poverty skyrocketed. Britain now has the highest child poverty, housing is broken, people are hounded to death, austerity is a permanent policy while the rich get richer. And migration? Same as it always was. This conversation is a distraction. No wonder the establishment love it."

source_type: Other (please specify)
Recent interviews, press releases and social media communication (from party, party leader and/or leading candidates)
text_snippet: "Mr President, we’re here today talking about defending the European Union against abuse of national vetoes. But to be honest, I’m much more interested in talking about defending national vetoes from the European Union. There is a sustained and deliberate campaign in this Parliament to undermine the right enshrined in the treaties to veto Council decisions, the very basis of the EU. Let me be clear: when it comes to foreign and defence policy, we should never, ever give those who dream of an imperial Europe what they want and abolish the veto. You can’t talk about a union of equals with the one breath and steamroll over small states with the next. Every Member State has its own interests, its own priorities, its own concerns. Of course, it might be difficult at times to find a solution that suits everyone. Sometimes states might use the veto as leverage, that’s politics. Talking about aid to Ukraine being blocked by the veto is nonsense. If Member States wanted to give macro-assistance they could give it, just not in the name of the Union. It’s time to stop this moral outrage, we’re not the United States of Europe. "

source_type: Recent interviews, press releases and social media communication (from party, party leader and/or leading candidates)
text_snippet: "Madam President, so here we go again. The latest in a barrage of new defence splurges, on top of the billions we’re already blowing on military research and production, we now have another 350 million on military procurement. The priorities could not be clearer. There’s never enough money for health, for housing, for education or public services. Working people have to tighten their belts. But as the act shows once more, there’s always money in the bank for bombs and bullets. If it’s more shells and missiles and cluster munitions and depleted uranium, money’s no object. We can always come up with a couple of billion, so working class people over here can go over and kill working class people over there. Well, you’ll never have our consent for that. The security we demand is the security of a decent job and a place to live. The defence we want is a defence of our rights, even as those rights are attacked by the governments of Europe and even as you sign up to bankroll slaughter. We reject a Europe that robs from the poor to boost the profits of the arms industry. Militarisation is never the answer."

source_type: Recent interviews, press releases and social media communication (from party, party leader and/or leading candidates)

It’s a scam: a massive transfer of public money straight into the pockets of the security industries. It’s billions being spent on walls and drones and spy tech, on contractors and consultants and border guards, on all of the expensive, deadly trappings of pushback, detention and surveillance. Billions taken from the things we need: health, housing, education and childcare. And this deal won’t stop migration. It probably won’t even slow it down. But it will kill plenty of people who have no choice but to leave their homes because of the policies we implement. And it will murder international law and the right to asylum."

source_type: Other (please specify)
text_snippet: Mr President, you can’t really go into a shop nowadays without everything you see being marketed as ‘climate neutral’. Whether it’s a pair of runners or a bunch of bananas, people are constantly bombarded by these generic claims, which actually don’t mean anything. We even have airlines selling ‘carbon-neutral’ flights. This has made a joke of the whole idea of conscious consumption and has lead shoppers into believing that they’re being codded, and they are, because generic environmental claims are inherently misleading, because they aren’t based on any standardised environmental performance. They’re often even specifically designed with a view to cod and intentionally fool the shoppers. So with this new legislation – and really thanks to the efforts of the Parliament – companies can no longer claim that their products are ‘climate neutral’, ‘eco-friendly’ or ‘green’. Carbon offsetting and calling them ‘carbon-neutral’ flights will also be outlawed. This is a really important step forward and fair play to the Parliament team for driving it forward.

source_type: Recent interviews, press releases and social media communication (from party, party leader and/or leading candidates)
text_snippet: Ireland’s strength on the international stage, as a neutral broker for peace in international institutions, is completely undermined by the Irish government aligning itself with belligerents in a geopolitical conflict. text_snippet: Raising the retirement age is not the solution; we need innovative financial strategies to support our pension systems without burdening older citizens. text_snippet: Regulated of soft drugs can reduce criminal justice burdens and increase public health safety through controlled use. source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: Integration within Europe must be based on mutual respect and an exchange of cultural values, rather than demanding complete cultural assimilation. The term 'accept' should be clearly defined - while it is reasonable to expect immigrants to respect and understand European cultures, it is crucial to emphasize that this does not mean they must forsake their own cultural identities. Equally, it is critically important that immigrants do not impose their own cultures upon others against their will. Effective integration fosters a shared respect for diverse cultural backgrounds, enhancing the social fabric of our communities.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I of course support the autonomy of women over their own bodies and acknowledge the argument that abortion access in cases such as rape, incest, and threats to the woman's health could be seen as a neccesity, there is a critical need for stricter regulatory measures. Recent incidents in Ireland, including a severe case in Limerick where a woman with an undiagnosed ectopic pregnancy was prescribed abortion pills, highlight the dangers of the current approach. This case resulted in life-threatening complications due to the lack of a mandatory ultrasound before prescribing such medication. It is imperative that legislation ensures the safety of women through mandatory pre-procedure ultrasounds to rule out conditions like ectopic pregnancies, which can be misdiagnosed as normal pregnancies by non-ultrasound methods. These measures would safeguard health and prevent potentially fatal outcomes, maintaining a balanced approach that respects both women's rights and their well-being.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: My stance on immigration is guided by a need for a balanced and community-involved approach. Reflecting on my own experiences abroad, I advocate for a system that embraces skilled migration and integrates immigrants in a manner that supports both their welfare and that of the local communities. Unregulated mass immigration, especially without local consultation and involvement, strains resources and can lead to significant social tension. We must prioritise a sustainable immigration policy that includes rigorous checks such as the verification of documentation at borders to maintain system integrity and fairness. Effective immigration should not only respect the cultural dynamics of host communities but also foster a harmonious and mutually beneficial integration.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I support gender quotas as a means to correct historical imbalances and promote diversity in governance, it is crucial that these measures do not overlook merit and competence. Selection should also be based on the ability to perform the role effectively, ensuring that positions are filled not just on the basis of gender but also on qualifications and skills. This approach ensures that while we strive for equality in representation, we also uphold the standards of excellence and efficiency in our governance structures.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: Investing in public transport through green taxes enhances sustainability and reduces urban congestion and pollution.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: Supporting renewable energy sources, despite initial costs, ensures long-term environmental sustainability and energy security. However, there must be buy-in from all across society, and not forced on anyone.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: Equality in social security rights is vital for the full integration of legally residing immigrants into society. This mirrors systems like Germany’s, where individuals contribute to and benefit from the system equitably, reflecting their legal status. It ensures that immigrants receive the same protections and benefits as nationals, contributing to and reinforcing overall community welfare. In my strong view such structured benefits affirm the principle that those who legally pay into the system should rightfully benefit from it.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While accountability is essential, our approach to crime should integrate understanding of underlying issues such as mental health and addiction, which can often drive criminal behaviour. Effective rehabilitation programs that address these root causes are crucial for reducing recidivism and promoting successful societal reintegration. It's vital to enforce laws firmly to maintain public confidence in our immigration systems and ensure community safety. Ensuring that all residents, regardless of status, are held to the same legal standards is key to a just and fair society. It is important to note that with regard to asylum seekers who commit serious offences, swift and decisive actions including deportation should be considered to protect community safety and ensure that our hospitality is not misused.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: Fiscal discipline within the EU is necessary for economic stability, but penalties should be fair and consider the unique circumstances of each member state.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: Granting the EU its own tax-raising powers could potentially enhance budgetary autonomy, supporting more unified and effective policy implementations across member states. However, this must be approached with very careful consideration to balance EU-wide interests with the sovereignty of individual nations. Sovereignty is a critical aspect of national identity and governance, and should not be compromised without thorough analysis, proper consensus, and, above all, the will of the people.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: As a scientist with a professional background in regulatory affairs, I completely agree that the EU must strengthen its regulation of AI. Proper regulations are vital for ensuring AI development is aligned with ethical standards and human rights, particularly as AI becomes integral to sectors like healthcare and finance. These regulations should prevent risks such as algorithmic bias and enhance public trust in technology. Although this approach might slow innovation, the benefits of a safer and more ethical framework far outweigh the potential delays in technological advancements.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: As a person who has lived and worked across Europe, I recognise the Euro's role in facilitating easier trade and economic integration among EU member states. However, its introduction significantly impacted living costs, particularly in Ireland where prices surged overnight. While the Euro promotes economic stability and integration, it limits national flexibility in economic crises and raises concerns about sovereignty and policy suitability for all member states. Balancing these factors, my view is that while the Euro has its benefits in terms of economic stability and integration, it also necessitates a critical evaluation of its impact on national sovereignty and the economic autonomy of member states like Ireland.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I support the principle of the EU providing financial assistance to member states in distress as it promotes solidarity and stability across the Union. However, the experience of Ireland with the Troika (IMF, ECB, European Commission) during the financial crisis raises significant concerns. The austerity measures imposed were to the severe detriment of the Irish people, cutting deeply into social services and economic freedom. Such conditions highlight the need for financial assistance that supports recovery without undermining national sovereignty or welfare. It’s crucial that future aid is accompanied by fair terms that protect the recipient country’s interests and promote sustainable economic recovery.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I support protective measures for our European farmers to shield them from unfair external competition, it is crucial that these protections do not lead to excessive food prices or market inefficiencies. We must find a balanced approach that sustains our agricultural sector while keeping food affordable. This includes advocating for targeted subsidies and promoting sustainable farming practices that benefit both the environment and our economy. Our goal should be to support our farmers and ensure high-quality, safe food production without compromising the cost of living for our families.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I understand the strategic advantages of the EU speaking with one voice on foreign policy issues to enhance our collective strength and global influence, we must also carefully consider the implications for national sovereignty. A unified stance should not come at the cost of suppressing individual member states' unique perspectives, especially on matters where national interests may diverge from broader EU policies. It is my strong view that we need a flexible framework that allows for a coordinated approach in areas of common interest, while still respecting the sovereignty and specific foreign policy needs of individual countries. This balance is crucial for maintaining both the effectiveness of EU foreign policy and the democratic integrity of its member states and their people.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I strongly oppose the mandatory proportional distribution of asylum-seekers across the EU, as it places undue burden on smaller states like Ireland, whose infrastructure cannot cope with such imposed quotas. The EU-Turkey deal exemplifies the pitfalls of such policies, where efforts to regulate migration flows have led to immense suffering for refugees trapped in limbo, and negligible returns to Turkey. This deal, while reducing arrivals, has not addressed the root causes and has instead exacerbated the humanitarian crisis on the ground. We need policies that support genuine refugee protection at the source and provide safe, legal pathways, rather than forcing countries to manage disproportionate and unsustainable burdens.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I support the strengthening of the EU's security and defence policy to address global threats and enhance collective security, I firmly oppose the concept of a united, federal Europe. Any enhancement of our defence capabilities must preserve the sovereignty of individual member states. It is crucial that integration efforts such as pooled resources, shared intelligence, and coordinated defence activities respect national autonomy and do not impinge on decision-making processes. By advocating for collaboration that upholds sovereignty, we can develop a robust defence posture that benefits all member states without moving towards a federalist structure.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I support the EU taking proactive measures in patrolling its borders to effectively manage migration flows and uphold security standards. Strong border controls are essential not only for regulating entry and exit but also for safeguarding all individuals from potential security threats that can emerge in any unmonitored movement, regardless of origin. Enhanced border security, including comprehensive checks and intelligence-sharing, will help ensure that those entering the EU do so legally and safely, contributing to a secure environment for both newcomers and current residents. This approach underlines the need for structured and lawful immigration processes.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I strongly oppose the proposed ban on internal combustion engine vehicles by 2035. While I recognize the goals of the European Green Deal to combat climate change, the plan to phase out combustion engines raises significant concerns about technological viability and the practical implications for consumers. Moreover, the regulatory overreach involved could infringe upon national sovereignty, dictating terms that may not align with the unique economic and infrastructural realities of individual member states. There are also serious safety concerns with current electric vehicle technologies, including risks of fires and operational failures. It is crucial that any transition be gradual and considerate of all stakeholder needs, ensuring technologies are reliable and infrastructure is adequately prepared. A balanced approach should ensure environmental benefits without compromising national autonomy or consumer safety.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I am firmly against the ongoing provision of military aid to Ukraine in the form of weapons and ammunition by the EU. It is my conviction that lasting peace stems from dialogue and diplomacy, not from the perpetuation of conflict through arms. The enduring war is not only a tragedy for those directly involved but also a concern for global stability, and it's troubling to consider how much the influence of the arms industry may be affecting decisions that lead to more violence. As someone seeking to represent you, I believe we must rigorously question the motives behind such militarisation and advocate for peaceful resolutions. If elected, I pledge to scrutinise these policies, push for transparency, and work towards sustainable peace efforts that prioritise the safety and well-being of all people over the profits of the few.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I do not support granting more powers to the European Parliament. Ireland joined the European Economic Community primarily for economic benefits, and the evolution into today's political union involves commitments that, in many ways, increasingly encroach on national sovereignty. While there have been undeniable benefits from our EU membership (and the EU has massively benefited from having Ireland as a member), the continuous transfer of powers from Dublin to Brussels risks diluting our national autonomy and the direct influence Irish citizens have over their own laws and policies. Strengthening European institutions should not come at the expense of member states' sovereignty. I am a firm believer that we must ensure that Ireland retains significant control over its own affairs to truly serve the interests of its people.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I acknowledge the economic and political benefits that European integration has brought to Ireland and other EU member states, such as enhanced market access and political stability, I remain cautious about further integration. The increasing centralisation of decision-making in Brussels can be concerning, particularly when it dilutes the decision-making of member states. A balanced approach is necessary, one that respects the sovereignty of individual nations while harnessing the collective strengths of the EU to address global challenges. This balance is crucial to ensuring that integration does not override local legislative powers or cultural identities.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I appreciate the need to streamline EU decision-making but remain cautious about reducing veto powers, which are vital for protecting national interests, especially for smaller states like Ireland. Reducing these powers risks overpowering smaller nations by larger member states in crucial policy decisions. However, I recognise the need for efficiency, particularly in urgent matters like crisis response. Any changes to veto rights must carefully balance efficiency with the ability to safeguard each member state’s sovereignty and unique interests.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I strongly oppose the idea of EU enlargement to include Ukraine at this stage. Granting membership to a country currently embroiled in an active conflict presents significant security concerns and compromises the stability of the EU. Moreover, Ukraine's well-documented struggles with corruption need to be robustly addressed before considering accession. It would be unfair to other candidate countries who have been engaged in long-standing efforts to align with EU standards, diligently working through the accession criteria. These nations have made substantial reforms to meet the EU's stringent requirements, and bypassing these for Ukraine could set a problematic precedent. Integrating a country at war, with massive systemic corruption issues, could also strain the EU’s resources and alter its geopolitical stance.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I support the principle of the EU taking measures to ensure that member states adhere to the rule of law, as it is fundamental to the union's integrity and the fair treatment of its citizens. However, such sanctions should be applied judiciously and transparently to avoid perceived overreach by EU institutions. It is crucial that these measures are not used as a form of punishment or to disproportionately target smaller member states, but rather to genuinely uphold democratic principles and the rule of law across the EU. Any actions taken must be balanced, fair, and include safeguards to protect the sovereignty of member states, ensuring that they are in line with the collective EU standards agreed upon by all members.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: I support a two-state solution as a viable path to achieving a lasting peace between Israel and Palestine. This stance aligns with international efforts to ensure equitable and peaceful coexistence, recognising the rights and sovereignties of both Israelis and Palestinians within internationally agreed borders. A two-state solution would not only help to resolve longstanding territorial disputes but also contribute to regional stability. However, this approach must be coupled with robust international mediation to ensure that both parties are committed to genuine negotiations and mutual respect. Furthermore, the EU (and indeed the wider international community) must play an active role in facilitating these discussions, ensuring that any agreement respects human rights and leads to a lasting peace. This solution should also consider the complexities of internal governance and corruption within the Palestinian territories, which can undermine peace efforts. Addressing these issues comprehensively is essential for creating a viable, peaceful two-state solution.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: As a scientist, I advocate for a balanced approach to pandemic management that equally considers public health, economic stability, and mental well-being. Restrictions must be scientifically justified, proportionate, and designed to minimise mental health issues, which have been exacerbated by some of the previous COVID-19 measures. Comprehensive support packages are essential to alleviate not only economic but also psychological impacts, helping with societal resilience. Transparency in the scientific rationale for these measures is crucial to maintain public trust and adherence. This strategy aims to safeguard physical and mental health, uphold personal freedoms, and support economic viability.

source_type: Other programmatic and official party documentation
text_snippet: While I acknowledge that Ireland's military funding is currently insufficient, which may necessitate careful increases to ensure national security and international obligations are met, I firmly believe in a balanced approach. Any increase in military spending should be judicious, transparent, and accompanied by rigorous audits to ensure that additional funds enhance capability without compromising the investment in vital public services like healthcare and education. We must ensure that the allocation towards defence is efficient, avoiding excessive expenditures, and is part of a broader strategy to maintain societal resilience and stability.
